1.content: The Empire of Tamriel is the ruling government that has united most of the continent under its banner, though its power has been waning in recent years. At its height, the Empire controlled nearly every province, from the deserts of Hammerfell to the icy plains of Skyrim. The Imperial City, in the heart of Cyrodiil, serves as its capital. The Imperial Legion enforces the Emperor's will, keeping the peace and defending against external threats. However, with the death of the last Septim and the rise of civil wars across the provinces, the Empire's grasp on power is slipping. Many wonder if Tamriel will ever return to a united state—or if it’s doomed to fall into chaos.
1.name: The Empire of Tamriel

2.content: The Aldmeri Dominion is a powerful alliance of the Altmer (High Elves) of Summerset Isles, the Bosmer (Wood Elves) of Valenwood, and the Khajiit of Elsweyr. Led by the Thalmor, an extremist faction of the High Elves, the Dominion seeks to reclaim the glory of Elven supremacy in Tamriel. They’ve already proven themselves a force to be reckoned with, clashing with the Empire in the Great War, which ended with the White-Gold Concordat—a peace treaty that favored the Dominion. The Thalmor continue to exert influence over the Empire, banning the worship of Talos and increasing their hold on various territories. Many fear the Dominion’s ambitions extend far beyond what they’ve already achieved.
2.name: The Aldmeri Dominion

3.content: The Thalmor are the political arm of the Aldmeri Dominion, made up mostly of Altmer who believe in the superiority of the Elven races. They’re strict, merciless, and driven by a desire to return the world to Elven rule, as it was in ancient times. The Thalmor’s influence stretches across Tamriel, particularly since the signing of the White-Gold Concordat. They’re feared for their secret police tactics and their ruthless enforcement of the ban on Talos worship. Those who defy them often disappear, and many whisper that the Thalmor’s ambitions are far more dangerous than they seem on the surface.
3.name: The Thalmor

4.content: The Dark Brotherhood is a shadowy organization of assassins who kill for gold, following the will of the Night Mother and Sithis, the void. Their network of hidden sanctuaries spans across Tamriel, though their influence has waned in recent years. The Brotherhood operates in secret, carrying out contracts for those who can pay the price. But it’s more than just murder for hire—it’s a deeply spiritual organization, guided by rituals and a twisted sense of family. They’ve been hunted and destroyed in some places, but the Brotherhood is never truly gone. As long as there are those who worship the Night Mother and Sithis, the Dark Brotherhood will continue to thrive in the shadows.
4.name: The Dark Brotherhood

5.content: The Companions are an ancient group of warriors based in Whiterun. They operate out of Jorrvaskr, the mead hall where they’ve trained and fought for centuries. The Companions are mercenaries for hire, but they pride themselves on honor and loyalty. They trace their lineage back to the legendary hero Ysgramor and his Five Hundred Companions who drove the elves from Skyrim. However, there’s more to the Companions than meets the eye. Some members of the inner circle, known as The Circle, harbor a dark secret—they are werewolves, gifted with the power of Hircine. While not all members are aware of this, those who join the Circle take on this ancient curse, balancing their sense of honor with the savage instincts of the beast within.
5.name: The Companions

6.content: The College of Winterhold is the last bastion of magical study in Skyrim, located in the frozen north in the ruined city of Winterhold. It’s a place for those who wish to hone their magical abilities, regardless of race or background. While magic is often viewed with suspicion by the people of Skyrim, the College has remained a sanctuary for mages who seek to understand and master their craft. Despite its isolation, the College houses vast libraries of arcane knowledge and is home to powerful wizards who guide the next generation of spellcasters. Though the College survived the Great Collapse that destroyed much of Winterhold, many believe it had something to do with the disaster—rumors the College has never been able to fully dispel.
6.name: The College of Winterhold

7.content: The Blades were once the protectors of the Empire, sworn to serve and defend the Dragonborn Emperors. However, with the fall of the Septim Dynasty, the Blades were scattered, hunted by the Thalmor for their loyalty to the old Empire. Now, they exist as a shadow of their former selves, secretly gathering information and searching for the Dragonborn, the one foretold to defeat Alduin, the World-Eater. The Blades have a long and storied history, and despite their current state, they remain dedicated to their ancient mission: protecting Tamriel from the return of the dragons. Some remnants of the Blades still operate in secret, waiting for the day they can rebuild.
7.name: The Blades

8.content: The Greybeards are an ancient order of monks who live atop High Hrothgar, the tallest mountain in Tamriel. They follow the Way of the Voice, a path of deep spiritual meditation centered around the power of the Thu’um—the ability to use the ancient language of the dragons to unleash powerful shouts. The Greybeards are pacifists, believing that the Thu’um should only be used in times of great need. They’ve isolated themselves from the rest of the world, choosing a life of quiet contemplation, though they train the Dragonborn in the use of the voice when the need arises. Their leader, Paarthurnax, is a wise and ancient dragon who has chosen to live among the Greybeards and aid them in their teachings.
8.name: The Greybeards

9.content: The Daedric Princes are powerful beings who dwell in the realms of Oblivion, each ruling over their own domain and influencing the mortal world in their own twisted ways. There are seventeen known Daedric Princes, each representing different aspects of existence—some are malevolent, like Molag Bal, the Prince of domination, or Mehrunes Dagon, the Prince of destruction. Others, like Azura or Meridia, are less cruel, but they all possess immense power. Mortals can summon them through rituals or stumble upon their influence through Daedric artifacts scattered across Tamriel. Interacting with Daedra often comes with a cost, as their motives are always complex and their rewards frequently dangerous.
9.name: Daedric Princes

10.content: The Nine Divines are the primary gods worshipped across the Empire, representing the Aedra who shaped Nirn and continue to influence the world of mortals. These include Akatosh, the Dragon God of Time and leader of the pantheon, Talos, the god of men and hero-god of the Empire, and other gods like Stendarr, Mara, and Kynareth. While their influence has been diminished in some parts of Tamriel due to the Thalmor’s ban on Talos worship, many still
10.name: The Nine Divines

11.content: The Altmer, commonly known as the High Elves, hail from the majestic Summerset Isles. Renowned for their golden skin and towering stature, the Altmer are the epitome of elven grace and sophistication. Their society is deeply rooted in magic, with many Altmer excelling in the arcane arts from a young age. The High Elves uphold a strict hierarchy, valuing purity of blood and mastery of magic above all else. Their capital, Alinor, stands as a testament to their architectural prowess and magical ingenuity. While the Altmer are admired for their intellect and magical abilities, their arrogance and isolationist tendencies often lead to tensions with other races. Nonetheless, their contributions to magic and scholarship are unparalleled, making them invaluable allies and formidable adversaries.
11.name: Altmer (High Elves)

12.content: The Argonians are a reptilian race native to the swamplands of Black Marsh. Adapted to their harsh, marshy environment, Argonians possess natural resistances to diseases and toxins, making them exceptional survivors. Their unique biology allows them to breathe both air and water, granting them unparalleled versatility in their homeland. The Argonians are deeply connected to the Hist, sentient trees that influence their culture and spirituality. Each Argonian is bonded to the Hist from birth, guiding their destiny and ensuring the survival of their people. Argonian society is organized into various tribes, each with its own customs and traditions. Known for their stealth and agility, Argonians often excel as scouts, assassins, and hunters. Despite facing prejudice and misunderstanding from other races, the Argonians remain proud and resilient, fiercely protective of their homeland and traditions.
12.name: Argonians

13.content: The Bosmer, or Wood Elves, inhabit the lush forests of Valenwood. Renowned for their exceptional archery skills and innate stealth, the Bosmer are masters of guerrilla warfare and survival in dense woodland environments. Their society is governed by the Green Pact, a sacred agreement that dictates their relationship with nature, forbidding them from harming plant life and mandating the consumption of only meat. This pact ensures the Bosmer live in harmony with their surroundings, using sustainable methods to sustain their communities. Bosmer are also known for their vibrant festivals, intricate craftsmanship, and deep spiritual connection to their ancestors and the forest. Despite their peaceful exterior, the Bosmer are formidable warriors when provoked, fiercely defending their homeland against any threats. Their agility and affinity for magic make them versatile allies and elusive foes.
13.name: Bosmer (Wood Elves)

14.content: The Bretons are a proud and resilient human race from the province of High Rock. Known for their mastery of both sword and sorcery, Bretons often serve as skilled knights, mages, and diplomats. Their mixed heritage of humans and elves grants them a natural aptitude for magic, making them some of the most talented spellcasters in Tamriel. Breton society is a blend of feudal traditions and scholarly pursuits, with many Bretons dedicating their lives to the study of magic, philosophy, and the arts. The Breton Empire has a long history of political maneuvering and military prowess, maintaining a delicate balance of power among the various noble houses. Bretons are admired for their chivalry, intelligence, and versatility, making them valuable allies and respected leaders across Tamriel.
14.name: Bretons

15.content: The Dunmer, or Dark Elves, hail from the volcanic province of Morrowind. With their ashen skin and piercing red eyes, Dunmer are instantly recognizable and often misunderstood by outsiders. Their homeland is characterized by ash-covered landscapes, towering mushroom forests, and ancient, mystical ruins. Dunmer society is deeply influenced by their complex history and the legacy of the Tribunal, a group of powerful leaders who once ruled Morrowind with divine authority. Despite the Tribunal's fall, Dunmer continue to uphold their traditions, valuing honor, resilience, and martial prowess. They are adept in both sorcery and warfare, often blending destructive magic with formidable combat skills. The Dunmer's strong sense of identity and loyalty to their homeland make them steadfast allies and tenacious defenders of their culture.
15.name: Dunmer (Dark Elves)

16.content: The Imperials are the human race from the heartland of Cyrodiil, the central province of the Empire of Tamriel. Known for their diplomatic skills, organizational prowess, and adaptability, Imperials often serve as emissaries, merchants, and military leaders throughout the Empire. The Imperial Legion is the backbone of their military might, maintaining order and defending the Empire's vast territories from internal and external threats. Imperial society is structured and hierarchical, with a strong emphasis on law, governance, and commerce. The Imperial City, capital of the Empire, is a bustling hub of trade, politics, and culture, reflecting the diversity and sophistication of Imperial civilization. Imperials are respected for their versatility, resilience, and commitment to the stability and prosperity of the Empire, making them reliable allies and formidable opponents.
16.name: Imperials

17.content: The Khajiit are a feline race from the desert province of Elsweyr. Known for their distinctive fur patterns and agile physiques, Khajiit are masters of stealth, agility, and commerce. Their society is organized into various clans, each with its own traditions, roles, and responsibilities. Khajiit culture is deeply intertwined with the phases of the moons, Jone and Jode, which influence their physical forms and magical abilities. This lunar connection grants the Khajiit a unique perspective on the world, blending spirituality with practicality. Renowned as skilled merchants and traders, Khajiit caravans traverse Tamriel, bringing goods and services to every corner of the continent. Their natural dexterity and keen senses make them exceptional thieves, scouts, and assassins. Despite facing prejudice and stereotypes, the Khajiit remain proud, resourceful, and fiercely loyal to their kin and traditions.
17.name: Khajiit

18.content: The Nords are a hardy human race from the frigid province of Skyrim. Renowned for their exceptional strength, resilience, and martial prowess, Nords are the quintessential warriors of Tamriel. Their society is built around clan loyalty, honor, and a deep reverence for their ancestors and the gods of old. Nords are fierce berserkers in battle, often wielding massive weapons and displaying incredible endurance against both human and supernatural foes. Their culture is rich with traditions, including grand feasts in mead halls, storytelling, and participation in fierce competitions like the Jarl's games. Nords are also deeply spiritual, believing in the power of the Divines and the spirits of the land. Despite their sometimes brash and aggressive demeanor, Nords are known for their bravery, loyalty, and unwavering commitment to protecting their homeland from any threat.
18.name: Nords

19.content: The Orcs, or Orsimer, hail from the rugged and mountainous regions of Orsinium, a stronghold that symbolizes their resilience and craftsmanship. Known for their muscular builds, green or gray skin, and formidable stature, Orcs are natural warriors and skilled blacksmiths. Their society is organized into clans, each led by a chieftain who upholds the clan's honor and traditions. Orcs value strength, honor, and craftsmanship, with many dedicating their lives to forging legendary weapons and armor. Despite facing prejudice and discrimination from other races, Orcs remain proud and steadfast, holding tightly to their cultural values and striving to prove their worth through acts of valor and excellence in battle. Their mastery of metalwork and combat makes them indispensable allies and fearsome adversaries in any conflict.
19.name: Orcs (Orsimer)

20.content: The Redguards are a resilient human race from the sun-soaked province of Hammerfell. Known for their dark skin, athletic builds, and exceptional combat skills, Redguards are revered as some of the finest warriors in Tamriel. Their society is steeped in a rich martial tradition, emphasizing honor, discipline, and personal prowess. Redguards excel in various forms of combat, from swordsmanship to archery, often incorporating unique martial arts techniques passed down through generations. Hammerfell is a land of harsh deserts, bustling ports, and fortified cities, reflecting the Redguards' adaptability and seafaring prowess. Redguard culture celebrates both individual achievement and communal strength, with festivals honoring great warriors and feats of bravery. Their steadfast determination and unwavering loyalty make Redguards formidable allies and respected figures across Tamriel.
20.name: Redguards

21.content: Skyrim is the northernmost province of Tamriel, home to the fierce and proud Nords. It’s a land of harsh winters, towering mountains, and ancient traditions. The people of Skyrim are as strong as the land they inhabit, known for their valor in battle and unwavering loyalty to their homeland. Whiterun, Windhelm, and Winterhold are some of the key cities, each with its own deep-rooted history and culture. In recent times, Skyrim has been torn apart by a civil war, with Ulfric Stormcloak leading the rebellion against the Imperial Empire, seeking to free Skyrim from Imperial control. This land is also home to ancient tombs and ruins, remnants of the Dragons, who once ruled the skies. Skyrim’s rugged beauty and strong, independent people make it a land of legend, where the spirit of the Nords can never be broken.
21.name: Skyrim

22.content: Cyrodiil is the heart of the Empire of Tamriel, a fertile and vast province that houses the Imperial City, the seat of power for the Empire. Known as the Heartland, Cyrodiil has a long history of warfare, diplomacy, and imperial dominance. The Imperial Legion calls this province home, and from here, they have led campaigns across Tamriel, enforcing the will of the Emperor. The fertile fields, dense forests, and temperate climate make Cyrodiil a prosperous and diverse land. The Imperial City, with its iconic White-Gold Tower, stands at the center of Tamriel, a symbol of the Empire’s reach and influence. Though Cyrodiil has known great prosperity, recent years have seen the Empire's power diminish, and with it, Cyrodiil's once unshakable dominance.
22.name: Cyrodiil

23.content: Morrowind is a mysterious and dangerous province located in the northeastern region of Tamriel, home to the Dunmer (Dark Elves). The land of Morrowind is dominated by the towering, volcanic Red Mountain and vast ashlands, making it one of the harshest environments in Tamriel. The Dunmer have built a society deeply rooted in their religion, led by the now-fallen Tribunal, who once ruled as living gods. Vvardenfell, a large volcanic island, is a key region within Morrowind and holds ancient Dwemer and Daedric ruins. Morrowind is also known for its unique flora and fauna, as well as its isolationist attitude. While the Dunmer have a strong sense of honor and tradition, Morrowind's society is often viewed with suspicion by outsiders. Despite its challenges, Morrowind remains a province rich in culture, magic, and history.
23.name: Morrowind

24.content: Elsweyr is the arid and exotic homeland of the Khajiit, a feline race known for their agility and cunning. The province is divided into two distinct regions: the northern Anequina, a hot, dry desert, and the southern Pelletine, a fertile savannah. Elsweyr’s culture is closely tied to the phases of the moons, Jone and Jode, which dictate the physical forms of the Khajiit and influence their spiritual practices. The province is often viewed with suspicion by outsiders due to its association with moon sugar trade, which many Khajiit use in rituals but is also the raw ingredient for Skooma, a dangerous narcotic. Despite its reputation, Elsweyr is a land of ancient traditions, bustling trade, and a unique connection to both nature and the divine.
24.name: Elsweyr

25.content: The Summerset Isles, also known as Alinor, are the ancestral homeland of the Altmer (High Elves), located far to the southwest of Tamriel. The islands are known for their stunning natural beauty and towering cities made of crystal and marble. The Altmer take great pride in their homeland, which they consider the pinnacle of civilization, art, and magical study. The capital city, Alinor, is a marvel of elven architecture and the seat of the Aldmeri Dominion. While the Summerset Isles are a place of great refinement and power, they are also highly insular, with the Altmer viewing outsiders—and even other elves—as inferior. The Thalmor, the ruling faction of the Dominion, extend their influence from these shores, enforcing their vision of elven supremacy across Tamriel.
25.name: Summerset Isles (Alinor)

26.content: Hammerfell is a rugged and arid province on the western coast of Tamriel, home to the resilient and formidable Redguards. The land is dominated by the vast Alik’r Desert, a harsh and unforgiving wasteland that has shaped the Redguards into some of the fiercest warriors in Tamriel. Hammerfell’s cities, such as Sentinel and Hegathe, are bastions of culture, trade, and seafaring prowess, making Hammerfell one of the key maritime powers of the continent. The Redguards are renowned for their exceptional swordsmanship, martial discipline, and independence, having successfully resisted both the Empire and the Aldmeri Dominion in the past. Hammerfell’s harsh environment has bred a people who value honor, loyalty, and the strength to overcome adversity.
26.name: Hammerfell

27.content: Valenwood is the dense, forested homeland of the Bosmer (Wood Elves), a land where towering trees and wild beasts dominate the landscape. The Bosmer live in harmony with nature, adhering to the Green Pact, which forbids them from harming the plants and trees of their homeland. Instead, the Bosmer are master hunters and archers, relying on the bounty of the forest for their survival. The towering Graht-oak trees serve as homes and fortresses for many of Valenwood’s people. While Valenwood is a place of beauty and mystery, it is also fraught with danger, as the Bosmer are fiercely protective of their home. Valenwood has long been part of the Aldmeri Dominion, aligning itself with the Altmer in their quest for elven dominance across Tamriel.
27.name: Valenwood

28.content: Black Marsh, also known as Argonia, is a vast and mysterious province in southeastern Tamriel, home to the enigmatic Argonians. The land is dominated by treacherous swamps, dense jungles, and hidden lagoons, making it nearly impossible for outsiders to navigate. The Argonians thrive in this harsh environment, relying on their natural resistance to disease and their deep connection to the Hist, the sentient trees that shape their culture and lives. Black Marsh has long been a land of isolation, resisting conquest by both the Empire and other forces due to its inhospitable terrain and the fiercely independent nature of its people. The Hist guide the Argonians in all things, and while Black Marsh may seem alien to outsiders, it is a sacred and vital land to its inhabitants.
28.name: Black Marsh (Argonia)

29.content: High Rock is a mountainous and fertile province in the northwest of Tamriel, home to the Bretons, a human race known for their skill in both magic and combat. The province is dotted with towering castles, fortified cities, and sprawling farmlands. Wayrest, Daggerfall, and Orsinium are some of the key cities in High Rock,
29.name: High Rock

30.content: The Oblivion Crisis was one of the darkest times in Tamriel’s history. In 3E 433, the Daedric Prince Mehrunes Dagon attempted to invade and conquer all of Tamriel, opening countless Oblivion Gates across the provinces. These portals allowed hordes of Daedra to flood into the mortal world, wreaking havoc on cities and villages alike. The crisis marked the end of the Septim Dynasty, as Emperor Uriel Septim VII and his heirs were assassinated, leaving the Empire in chaos. However, a hero known as the Champion of Cyrodiil emerged, working alongside the last known Septim, Martin Septim, to close the gates and ultimately banish Mehrunes Dagon back to Oblivion. Martin sacrificed himself to channel the power of Akatosh, ending the crisis and saving Tamriel from destruction. The event left lasting scars on the Empire, leading to the weakening of Imperial authority and a new era of uncertainty.
30.name: The Oblivion Crisis

31.content: The Red Year refers to the catastrophic eruption of Red Mountain in 4E 5, a disaster that devastated Morrowind, particularly the island of Vvardenfell. The explosion and resulting ash storms destroyed cities, villages, and ancient ruins, causing the deaths of countless Dunmer. The eruption also caused widespread displacement, with many Dunmer fleeing to other provinces, such as Skyrim and Solstheim. The Argonians, long held as slaves by the Dunmer, seized the opportunity to invade southern Morrowind during the chaos, adding to the region's turmoil. The Red Year marked the end of Morrowind's power and prestige, and the once-proud Dunmer people were left scattered and broken. The disaster was a turning point in Tamrielic history, altering the landscape of power in the northeast.
31.name: The Red Year (The Eruption of Red Mountain)

32.content: The Great War was a brutal conflict fought between the Aldmeri Dominion, led by the Thalmor, and the Empire of Tamriel. It began in 4E 171 when the Dominion, seeking to assert its dominance, invaded Cyrodiil and Hammerfell, forcing the Empire into a desperate struggle for survival. The war lasted for five years, with battles fought across the heartlands of Tamriel. The war culminated in the Battle of the Red Ring, where the Empire managed to drive the Dominion forces out of the Imperial City. However, despite this victory, the war ended with the signing of the White-Gold Concordat, a peace treaty that heavily favored the Dominion. The treaty banned the worship of Talos, a condition that infuriated many citizens, particularly in Skyrim. The Great War left the Empire weakened, with many seeing the treaty as a sign of Imperial submission to the Thalmor, fueling unrest in the provinces.
32.name: The Great War

33.content: The Dragon Crisis refers to the sudden and devastating return of the dragons in the early Fourth Era, most notably in Skyrim. These ancient, powerful creatures, led by the World-Eater Alduin, were believed to have been long extinct, but they returned to wreak havoc across the province. Alduin’s goal was to fulfill an ancient prophecy by devouring the world. The people of Skyrim were caught unprepared, as the dragons laid waste to towns and cities. In the midst of this chaos, a new Dragonborn emerged—an individual blessed by the gods with the ability to absorb a dragon’s soul and use the power of the Thu’um (the dragon shout). With the guidance of the Greybeards and the Blades, the Dragonborn set out to stop Alduin and put an end to the dragon threat. The crisis marked a turning point in Skyrim’s history and reawakened long-forgotten myths and prophecies about the return of dragons.
33.name: The Dragon Crisis

34.content: The Warp in the West, also known as the Miracle of Peace, was an event in 3E 417 that reshaped the political landscape of the Iliac Bay region in western Tamriel. Before the Warp, the region was divided into dozens of warring kingdoms, including Daggerfall, Wayrest, and Orsinium. In a single day, due to a mysterious and powerful event, these many petty kingdoms were consolidated into just four dominant powers, all ruling over the previously fragmented lands. This miraculous change occurred without any major bloodshed, leading to an era of unprecedented peace in the region. The true cause of the Warp remains a mystery, but many speculate it was the result of powerful magic, possibly involving the manipulation of time or even divine intervention. The Warp in the West left a lasting impact on the politics of western Tamriel, with the new powers asserting their influence far beyond the Iliac Bay.
34.name: The Warp in the West (The Miracle of Peace)

35.content: The Five Year War was a protracted conflict between the Bosmer of Valenwood and the Khajiit of Elsweyr in the mid-Second Era. The war was sparked by territorial disputes over the lush borderlands between Valenwood and Elsweyr, lands both peoples sought to control. The Bosmer, masters of guerrilla warfare, clashed with the swift and agile Khajiit in a brutal series of skirmishes and ambushes that lasted for five long years. Both sides suffered heavy casualties, and the war had devastating effects on the environment, as the Bosmer invoked the Green Pact and engaged in savage acts of warfare. The war ended with a stalemate, but the tensions between the Bosmer and Khajiit remained high, fostering a legacy of distrust between the two races.
35.name: The Five Year War

36.content: The Tribunal was a group of god-like rulers who once governed Morrowind with divine authority. Comprised of Vivec, Almalexia, and Sotha Sil, the Tribunal were revered by the Dunmer for centuries. However, their power came not from the Divines, but from their use of the Heart of Lorkhan, a powerful artifact that they used to achieve their godhood. Over time, the Tribunal's influence began to wane, especially following the events of the Nerevarine Prophecy, when the Nerevarine exposed the truth of their rise to power. The destruction of the Heart of Lorkhan and the death of Dagoth Ur further destabilized the Tribunal, leading to their downfall. With their power gone and their lies exposed, the Tribunal fell from grace, leaving the Dunmer to seek new spiritual guidance. The fall of the Tribunal marked the end of an era in Morrowind’s history, and the province has never fully recovered from the loss of its god-kings.
36.name: The Fall of the Tribunal

37.content: The First Battle of Red Mountain was a pivotal event in the First Era, fought between the Chimer (who later became the Dunmer) and the Dwemer, who sought control of the Heart of Lorkhan. Led by the hero Nerevar and his allies, the Chimer triumphed over the Dwemer, but at a great cost. The Dwemer vanished from Tamriel in the aftermath of the battle, their fate still a mystery to this day. The battle also led to the rise of the Tribunal, who used the Heart to achieve godhood.
37.name: The First and Second Battles of Red Mountain

38.content: Dagoth Ur was once a trusted friend and ally of Lord Nerevar, but after the First Battle of Red Mountain, he betrayed his people and claimed the power of the Heart of Lorkhan for himself. Dagoth Ur sought to establish himself as a god and rebuild the lost Sixth House, leading to centuries of conflict in Morrowind. After the fall of the Tribunal’s power, Dagoth Ur used the Heart to gain immortality, planning to overthrow both the Tribunal and the Empire. His presence corrupted the land around Red Mountain, and he was eventually defeated by the Nerevarine. Though Dagoth Ur is gone, his dark legacy lingers, and the Sixth House’s influence can still be felt in the shadows of Morrowind.
38.name: Dagoth Ur

39.content: The Tribunal, also known as the Almsivi, were the three living gods of Morrowind: Vivec, Almalexia, and Sotha Sil. They achieved divinity through their connection to the Heart of Lorkhan, using its power to establish themselves as deities. For centuries, the Tribunal ruled Morrowind, defending it from external threats and guiding the Dunmer people. However, their godhood was built on betrayal, as they turned against their friend Nerevar to claim the Heart’s power. The Tribunal’s reign ended with the rise of Dagoth Ur and the fulfillment of the Nerevarine Prophecy, which saw their divine powers stripped away. Despite their fall, the Tribunal left an indelible mark on Morrowind’s history and culture.
39.name: The Tribunal

40.content: Indoril Nerevar was a legendary hero of Morrowind, known for uniting the Chimer and leading them to victory over the Dwemer in the First Battle of Red Mountain. Nerevar was a wise and just leader, deeply respected by his people and his allies. However, after the battle, his closest companions—Vivec, Almalexia, and Sotha Sil—betrayed him, using the Heart of Lorkhan to achieve godhood for themselves. Nerevar’s death marked the beginning of the Tribunal’s reign, but his legacy lived on through the Nerevarine Prophecy, which foretold the return of a reincarnated Nerevar to set things right and bring down the Tribunal.
40.name: Nerevar

41.content: The Dwemer, also known as the Deep Elves, were an advanced and mysterious race of elves who once thrived in Morrowind and other parts of Tamriel. They were renowned for their technological and magical prowess, creating intricate machines, automatons, and powerful artifacts. However, the Dwemer vanished suddenly and completely during the First Battle of Red Mountain, leaving behind only their ruins and inventions. Their disappearance remains one of Tamriel’s greatest mysteries, with many theories but no definitive answers. The Dwemer’s technology, particularly their Tonality, continues to intrigue and baffle scholars, adventurers, and treasure hunters to this day.
41.name: Dwemer

42.content: The Heart of Lorkhan is one of the most powerful and enigmatic artifacts in all of Tamriel. It is said that the god Lorkhan used the Heart to create the mortal world, but the other gods betrayed him, tearing the Heart from his body and casting it down to Nirn. The Dwemer discovered the Heart deep within Red Mountain, and their attempt to harness its power led to their mysterious disappearance. Later, the Tribunal used the Heart to achieve their godhood, though they kept this secret from the Dunmer people. The Heart’s power was finally severed by the Nerevarine, ending the reign of the Tribunal and the threat of Dagoth Ur.
42.name: Heart of Lorkhan

43.content: The Nerevarine Prophecy foretold the return of Nerevar, reincarnated to defeat Dagoth Ur and restore balance to Morrowind. According to the prophecy, the Nerevarine would be born under certain signs, cure the Corprus disease, and unite the Great Houses and the Ashlanders against Dagoth Ur. The fulfillment of the prophecy would also mark the end of the Tribunal’s rule and the downfall of the Sixth House. Many doubted the prophecy until it was ultimately fulfilled by the Nerevarine, who defeated Dagoth Ur and severed the connection to the Heart of Lorkhan, changing Morrowind forever.
43.name: Nerevarine Prophecy

44.content: Vivec is one of the three gods of the Tribunal, revered by the Dunmer of Morrowind. As both a warrior and a poet, Vivec embodies the duality of strength and wisdom. He resides in the grand city of Vivec, a city built in his honor, where he served as a ruler and a protector of Morrowind. Vivec played a central role in the First Battle of Red Mountain and later betrayed his friend Nerevar to claim godhood for himself, alongside Almalexia and Sotha Sil. Despite his divine status, Vivec has always been more involved with mortal affairs than the other members of the Tribunal, and he is known for his cryptic teachings and complex personality.
44.name: Vivec

45.content: Almalexia was the most revered of the Tribunal gods, known as the Mother of Morrowind and the patron goddess of the Dunmer people. As the most outwardly benevolent of the Tribunal, she was deeply involved in the daily lives of her followers, offering guidance, healing, and protection. However, behind her nurturing image lay a dark ambition. Almalexia played a part in the betrayal of Lord Nerevar, seizing the Heart of Lorkhan’s power to become a god. As the Tribunal’s power waned, Almalexia became more desperate to hold on to her divinity, eventually leading to her madness and downfall.
45.name: Almalexia

46.content: Sotha Sil was the most enigmatic member of the Tribunal, a genius sorcerer and craftsman who sought to unlock the mysteries of the universe. Known as the Mystic, Sotha Sil was responsible for many of Morrowind’s technological and magical advancements, creating the wondrous Clockwork City, a hidden realm of machines and magic. Like Vivec and Almalexia, Sotha Sil used the power of the Heart of Lorkhan to ascend to godhood, but he was always more distant from the world, preferring to dwell in his own creations. As the Tribunal’s power faded, Sotha Sil became increasingly reclusive, eventually meeting his end in his own Clockwork City.
46.name: Sotha Sil

47.content: The Green Pact is a sacred agreement between the Bosmer of Valenwood and the forest itself. According to the pact, Bosmer are forbidden from harming the trees and plants of Valenwood, instead living in harmony with nature. In return, they are granted the blessings of the forest, including the ability to shape wood and communicate with the wild. The Green Pact also dictates that the Bosmer are to consume only meat, leading to their practice of cannibalism in warfare, where they eat their fallen enemies to honor the pact. This unique relationship with nature has shaped Bosmer society for centuries, making them one of the most deeply connected races to the land they inhabit.
47.name: Green Pact

48.content: Daggerfall is one of the most powerful kingdoms in the region of High Rock. The city itself sits on the western coast of Tamriel, overlooking the Iliac Bay, and has long been a center of trade, politics, and military strength. Ruled by the royal family of House Cameron, Daggerfall has played a pivotal role in the politics of the Bretons and the entire Iliac Bay region. Daggerfall is known for its skilled knights and formidable navy, making it a force to be reckoned with among the many kingdoms of High Rock.
48.name: Daggerfall

49.content: Wayrest is a prosperous kingdom in High Rock, located along the Bjoulsae River on the shores of the Iliac Bay. It has long been a center of trade and commerce, and its ruling family, House Gardener, has maintained a strong influence over the region. Known for its skilled diplomats and merchant class, Wayrest has often served as a mediator between the rival kingdoms of High Rock. Its location along the Iliac Bay has made it a vital port city, and its wealth and influence have grown as a result.
49.name: Wayrest

50.content: Orsinium is the ancestral homeland of the Orcs (Orsimer), located in the rugged mountains between High Rock and Hammerfell. Throughout history, Orsinium has been built and destroyed many times, as the Orcs have long struggled for recognition and acceptance from the other races of Tamriel. Orsinium is a symbol of Orcish unity and resilience, as it serves as both a city and a sanctuary for their people. The Orsimer have a deep connection to their home, even when it is in ruins, and they continue to rebuild Orsinium whenever it falls. Though often viewed with suspicion by outsiders, the Orcs are a proud and honorable people, known for their strength and craftsmanship.
50.name: Orsinium

51.content: The Iliac Bay is a large and strategic body of water located between High Rock and Hammerfell. It has long been a center of commerce and conflict, as the surrounding kingdoms—Daggerfall, Wayrest, Sentinel, and Orsinium—have vied for control over its valuable trade routes and resources. The Iliac Bay region is known for its political complexity, with many noble houses and factions constantly maneuvering for power and influence. The Warp in the West, also known as the Miracle of Peace, drastically changed the political landscape of the Iliac Bay, consolidating the power of its major kingdoms.
51.name: Iliac Bay

52.content: The Thu’um, also known as the Voice or Dragon Shout, is an ancient form of magic used by those who can speak in the Dragon Language. It grants the speaker the ability to harness the power of the dragons, allowing them to perform incredible feats such as summoning storms, unleashing forceful blasts, or even controlling time. The Greybeards, monks who live on High Hrothgar, are the most skilled masters of the Thu’um, dedicating their lives to its study and teaching the Dragonborn how to wield this power. The Nords believe the Thu’um is a gift from the gods, and it has played a significant role in their history, particularly during the Dragon War.
52.name: Thu’um

53.content: The Dragonborn, or Dovahkiin, is a mortal blessed with the soul and power of a dragon. They have the unique ability to absorb the souls of dragons and master the Thu’um (Dragon Shouts). The Dragonborn plays a central role in many prophecies, including the defeat of Alduin, the World-Eater. Throughout history, there have been many Dragonborn, some of whom ruled as Emperors of Tamriel, but the most famous is the hero who emerged during the Dragon Crisis in Skyrim. The Dragonborn is viewed as the ultimate hero of mankind, destined to defeat the dragons and restore balance to the world.
53.name: Dragonborn

54.content: Alduin, the World-Eater, is an ancient and powerful dragon prophesied to bring about the end of the world. He is said to have been the first dragon created by Akatosh and was worshipped as a god by the Nords in ancient times. However, Alduin’s true purpose is to devour the world and begin the cycle of time anew. During the Dragon War, Alduin was defeated and banished to another realm, but he returned during the Fourth Era, leading a new wave of dragon attacks in Skyrim. The Dragonborn was the only one capable of defeating him, and their battle on the Throat of the World determined the fate of Tamriel.
54.name: World-Eater Alduin

55.content: Dragons are ancient, powerful, and intelligent beings that once ruled over Tamriel in the distant past. Their language, the Thu’um, holds immense power, and they are able to bend the world to their will with their shouts. Though dragons were thought to be extinct, they re-emerged during the Fourth Era, led by Alduin, the World-Eater. The people of Skyrim were the first to face the renewed dragon threat, with cities and villages falling to their fiery attacks. Only the Dragonborn has the power to permanently slay a dragon by absorbing its soul. Dragons are feared and revered across Tamriel, and their return has reshaped the course of history.
55.name: Dragons

56.content: The White-Gold Concordat is the peace treaty signed between the Empire of Tamriel and the Aldmeri Dominion following the Great War. The treaty brought an end to the devastating conflict, but at a great cost to the Empire. The Concordat’s most controversial term was the banning of Talos worship throughout the Empire, a concession demanded by the Thalmor. This ban has caused unrest, particularly in Skyrim, where many Nords revere Talos as a divine hero. While the White-Gold Concordat secured peace, it also diminished the Empire’s power and allowed the Dominion to extend its influence over much of Tamriel.
56.name: White-Gold Concordat

57.content: The Battle of the Red Ring was the decisive conflict that ended the Great War between the Empire of Tamriel and the Aldmeri Dominion. Fought in the Imperial City, the battle saw the Imperial Legion, under the command of General Tullius, launch a daring assault to retake the city from the Dominion forces. The Empire emerged victorious, driving the Dominion out of the city and dealing a critical blow to their war effort. However, despite the victory, the White-Gold Concordat that followed the battle heavily favored the Dominion, leading to lasting resentment among the Empire’s citizens.
57.name: Battle of the Red Ring

58.content: Solstheim is a remote and harsh island located to the northeast of Skyrim and northwest of Morrowind. Known for its rugged terrain, Solstheim has been influenced by both Nordic and Dunmer culture. After the Red Year, many Dunmer refugees fled to Solstheim, and the island came under House Redoran’s control. The island is also known for its connection to the Daedric Prince Miraak, the first Dragonborn, who once ruled over the island. Solstheim is a land of mystery, danger, and ancient magic, where the power of the All-Maker and the secrets of the Dwemer remain hidden in its volcanic landscape.
58.name: Solstheim

59.content: Martin Septim was the last known heir of the Septim Dynasty and played a pivotal role in ending the Oblivion Crisis. A humble priest of Akatosh, Martin was revealed to be the illegitimate son of Emperor Uriel Septim VII after the death of the Imperial family. With the help of the Champion of Cyrodiil, Martin took up his rightful place as Emperor and sacrificed himself to summon the Avatar of Akatosh, banishing Mehrunes Dagon and saving Tamriel from destruction. Martin’s heroic actions ended the Septim bloodline, ushering in the Fourth Era.
59.name: Martin Septim

60.content: The Septim Dynasty was founded by the legendary Tiber Septim (later deified as Talos) and ruled the Empire of Tamriel for centuries. The Septims were known for uniting Tamriel under Imperial rule and bringing about the Third Era, a time of relative peace and prosperity. The Dynasty came to an abrupt end with the assassination of Emperor Uriel Septim VII and the sacrifice of his son, Martin Septim, during the Oblivion Crisis. Their legacy is one of strength, conquest, and the establishment of Tamriel’s greatest empire.
60.name: Septim Dynasty

61.content: Emperor Uriel Septim VII was one of the last rulers of the Septim Dynasty and is best remembered for his role in the events leading up to the Oblivion Crisis. A wise and experienced ruler, Uriel sought to maintain peace and stability across the Empire. However, his reign ended in tragedy when he was assassinated by the Mythic Dawn, a cult dedicated to Mehrunes Dagon. Uriel’s death triggered the events of the Oblivion Crisis, leaving the Empire without an heir and plunging Tamriel into chaos. His final act was entrusting the Champion of Cyrodiil with the task of finding his lost son, Martin Septim.
61.name: Emperor Uriel Septim VII

62.content: The Champion of Cyrodiil is the legendary hero who played a pivotal role in ending the Oblivion Crisis. After the assassination of Emperor Uriel Septim VII, the Champion rose to prominence, defeating the forces of Mehrunes Dagon, closing the Oblivion Gates, and ensuring the survival of Tamriel. The Champion worked closely with Martin Septim to recover the Amulet of Kings and prevent Dagon’s invasion. Their bravery and determination were instrumental in saving the Empire, and their deeds are celebrated throughout the land.
62.name: Champion of Cyrodiil

63.content: Oblivion Gates were portals that connected Tamriel to the Daedric realm of Oblivion, allowing the Daedra to invade during the Oblivion Crisis. These fiery, ominous gates opened across the Empire, unleashing hordes of Daedric creatures upon the land. The only way to close the gates was to recover the Sigil Stones from within the Daedric realms, a dangerous task that fell to the Champion of Cyrodiil. Closing the Oblivion Gates was key to stopping Mehrunes Dagon’s invasion and saving Tamriel from certain destruction.
63.name: Oblivion Gates

64.content: Mehrunes Dagon is one of the Daedric Princes, known as the Prince of Destruction, Change, Revolution, and Ambition. His sphere of influence encompasses the destructive forces of nature, calamity, and the constant upheaval of mortal and immortal lives alike. He is often associated with catastrophic events, rebellions, and the very destruction of Nirn itself. Dagon's realm, the Deadlands, is a fiery, volcanic world of perpetual storms and molten rivers. He played a central role in the Oblivion Crisis, attempting to invade Tamriel through Oblivion Gates. His followers, the Mythic Dawn, sought to bring his rule to Tamriel.
64.name: Mehrunes Dagon

65.content: Sigil Stones are powerful Daedric artifacts used by Mehrunes Dagon to anchor Oblivion Gates in Tamriel during the Oblivion Crisis. These stones serve as a conduit between the planes of Oblivion and Nirn, allowing the Daedra to breach Tamriel's defenses. Sigil Stones are also used by powerful mages to enchant items with unique, potent magical effects, tapping into their raw Daedric power. Removing a Sigil Stone from an Oblivion Gate disrupts the link between realms and causes the gate to collapse.
65.name: Sigil Stones

66.content: The Amulet of Kings is a sacred relic of Tamriel, passed down through the generations of the Septim Dynasty. It symbolizes the Covenant between the dragon god Akatosh and the Empire of Cyrodiil, representing the divine right of the Septim line to rule. The Amulet is a powerful artifact that can only be worn by those with Dragonblood, a symbol of the ruler's connection to the gods. Its main purpose is to maintain the barriers between Oblivion and Nirn by lighting the Dragonfires in the Temple of the One, which prevents Daedric Princes from invading Tamriel. During the Oblivion Crisis, the Amulet of Kings played a pivotal role in closing the gates to Oblivion for good.
66.name: Amulet of Kings

67.content: The Mythic Dawn is a secretive Daedric cult dedicated to Mehrunes Dagon, the Daedric Prince of Destruction. Led by Mankar Camoran, the cult played a critical role in the Oblivion Crisis by assassinating the Emperor Uriel Septim VII and his heirs, paving the way for Dagon's invasion of Tamriel. The cult follows the teachings of the Mysterium Xarxes, a book written by Dagon himself. Members of the Mythic Dawn are fanatical in their devotion, believing that the destruction of the world will herald a new dawn under Dagon's rule.
67.name: Mythic Dawn

68.content: Akatosh, also known as the Dragon God of Time, is one of the most prominent Aedra and a central figure in the pantheon of the Nine Divines. He is the god of time, endurance, and the principal deity of the Imperial Cult. As the chief deity of the Divines, Akatosh plays a vital role in maintaining the balance of Nirn. The Amulet of Kings and the Dragonfires are tied to Akatosh, representing his covenant with the rulers of Tamriel to protect the realm from Daedric invasion. He is often depicted as a great dragon and is revered by both humans and elves.
68.name: Akatosh

69.content: The All-Maker is the central deity of the Skaal people of Solstheim, representing the life force that flows through all living things. The All-Maker is a god of creation, balance, and nature, embodying the harmonious relationship between all elements of the world. According to Skaal belief, the All-Maker created the world and everything in it, and to honor him, the Skaal live in balance with nature, respecting the natural order and the spirits that inhabit the world. The All-Maker's influence is deeply tied to the land and its people, guiding the Skaal in their spiritual practices and way of life.
69.name: All-Maker

70.content: The Daedric Princes are powerful god-like beings who reside in the planes of Oblivion. Unlike the Aedra, the Daedra did not participate in the creation of Nirn and instead chose to remain separate from it. There are 17 recognized Daedric Princes, each ruling over their own distinct realm of Oblivion, embodying various aspects of existence and emotions, from madness (Sheogorath) to domination (Molag Bal) and destruction (Mehrunes Dagon). Though often feared, Daedric Princes can also be revered by mortals, especially through dark cults or individuals seeking power. Their influence is both a source of temptation and chaos across Tamriel.
70.name: Daedric Prince

71.content: Miraak was the first Dragonborn, a powerful figure who rebelled against the dragons who ruled over mortal men. Once a dragon priest, Miraak discovered his own Dragonborn power and sought to overthrow the dragons, ultimately making a pact with the Daedric Prince Hermaeus Mora. Trapped in Mora's realm of Apocrypha for centuries, Miraak learned forbidden knowledge and grew in power, intending to return to Nirn to conquer it. His ambitions came into conflict with the Last Dragonborn, resulting in a dramatic confrontation on the island of Solstheim. Miraak's mastery of the Thu’um, including his ability to Bend Will, made him one of the most dangerous threats of his time.
71.name: Miraak

72.content: House Redoran is one of the five Great Houses of Morrowind, known for its strong warrior culture and commitment to martial honor. Redoran values duty, piety, and honor above all, and its members are often regarded as noble warriors and protectors of Morrowind. Based in the city of Ald'ruhn on the volcanic island of Vvardenfell, House Redoran has traditionally played a vital role in the defense of Morrowind from both external and internal threats. The house is heavily involved in the politics of the Tribunal and has been a staunch opponent of the Aldmeri Dominion's influence in Tamriel.
72.name: House Redoran

73.content: General Tullius is the commanding officer of the Imperial Legion in Skyrim, tasked with quelling the Stormcloak Rebellion led by Ulfric Stormcloak. A native of Cyrodiil, Tullius is a seasoned military leader known for his strategic mind and dedication to preserving the Empire. He was sent to Skyrim during the Skyrim Civil War to restore order and enforce the Empire’s laws, which include the ban on Talos worship, an issue that has fueled much of the rebellion. Tullius is pragmatic, loyal to the Emperor, and unafraid to make difficult decisions in the face of growing opposition from both the Stormcloaks and the Thalmor.
73.name: General Tullius

74.content: Talos worship refers to the veneration of Tiber Septim, also known as Talos, the god of war and governance, who ascended to become the Ninth Divine. Revered particularly by the Nords of Skyrim, Talos is a symbol of strength and independence. However, under the White-Gold Concordat, a peace treaty between the Empire and the Aldmeri Dominion, Talos worship was outlawed to appease the Thalmor, who see Talos worship as a heresy. This ban has caused significant unrest in Skyrim, with many Nords viewing it as an attack on their heritage and religious freedom. Talos worship remains a central issue in the Skyrim Civil War, fueling the conflict between the Empire and the Stormcloaks.
74.name: Talos Worship

75.content: The Throat of the World is the highest mountain in Tamriel, located in Skyrim. It is a place of great historical and spiritual significance, particularly for the Greybeards, an order of monks who follow the Way of the Voice. Atop the mountain lies High Hrothgar, where the Greybeards live in quiet meditation, training the Dragonborn in the ancient power of the Thu’um. The Throat of the World is also where Paarthurnax, an ancient dragon who turned against his kind, resides. The mountain is closely tied to the history of dragons and the power of the Voice, making it a crucial location for the Dragonborn and their quest.
75.name: Throat of the World

76.content: High Hrothgar is a secluded monastery located near the summit of the Throat of the World, the highest peak in Tamriel. It is the home of the Greybeards, a reclusive order of monks who devote their lives to mastering the Way of the Voice, or the Thu’um. The monastery is a place of meditation and spiritual reflection, where the Dragonborn are summoned to learn and harness their power. High Hrothgar holds great significance in the history of Skyrim, and only those truly called by the Greybeards dare to make the arduous pilgrimage to its sacred halls. Paarthurnax, an ancient dragon who renounced his destructive ways, resides further up the mountain.
76.name: High Hrothgar

77.content: The Dragon Language, or Dovahzul, is the ancient tongue of dragons, used to invoke powerful magic in the form of shouts, or Thu’um. Each word in the language carries immense power, and when spoken by a Dragonborn or a dragon, it can alter reality itself. The language is composed of syllables that reflect the nature of the world, life, and power. Dragonborn, individuals blessed with the soul of a dragon, are able to learn and harness these words of power. Throughout Skyrim, the Dragon Language is found etched in ancient ruins, often guarded by powerful dragons or hidden deep within the landscape.
77.name: Dragon Language

78.content: The Warp in the West, also known as the Miracle of Peace, was a mysterious and sudden event that took place in the region of Iliac Bay. It involved a Dragon Break, a phenomenon where the flow of time becomes fractured. During this event, multiple conflicting outcomes occurred simultaneously across the region, allowing several different kingdoms and powers to claim victory in the political struggle over the control of the Numidium, a powerful golem. Despite the chaos, the Warp in the West resulted in a sudden and peaceful resolution to conflicts in the region, with the Empire consolidating power and many local rulers retaining autonomy. The Warp remains one of the most puzzling and debated events in Tamrielic history.
78.name: Warp in the West

79.content: House Gardener was an influential noble family in High Rock, known for their role in the politics of the Breton kingdoms. They ruled over various territories in the region and were known as strong feudal lords with connections to other prominent Breton houses. House Gardener is particularly remembered for its stewardship of the city of Wayrest and its involvement in the region's power struggles during the Middle Era. Though their influence waned over time, they are still respected in the annals of Breton history as one of the noble houses that shaped the politics and culture of High Rock.
79.name: House Gardener

80.content: The Bjoulsae River is a major waterway flowing through High Rock and Hammerfell, eventually feeding into the Iliac Bay. It has long served as a vital trade route, connecting the inland regions of these provinces to the coast. The river is known for its turbulent waters and is a lifeline for the Bretons and Redguards who live along its banks. Villages and towns have sprung up along the river, fostering a rich cultural exchange between the two peoples, as well as providing key points for commerce and trade. The Bjoulsae River is also a significant geographical landmark in the region, shaping both the natural and political landscapes of High Rock and Hammerfell.
80.name: Bjoulsae River

81.content: House Camoran is one of the oldest and most influential noble families of the Bosmer (Wood Elves) in Valenwood. The Camoran Dynasty ruled over Valenwood for centuries, and members of the house were key figures in the politics of Tamriel. One of the most infamous members, Mankar Camoran, was the leader of the Mythic Dawn, a Daedric cult responsible for the assassination of Emperor Uriel Septim VII and the Oblivion Crisis. Mankar Camoran’s teachings and the use of the Mysterium Xarxes marked House Camoran as a name associated with both great nobility and dangerous heresy. The legacy of House Camoran is a mix of grandeur and dark influence in Tamrielic history.
81.name: House Camoran

82.content: The Clockwork City is a mechanical realm created by Sotha Sil, one of the Tribunal gods of Morrowind. It is a miniature world of gears, cogs, and machinery that exists in its own plane, representing Sotha Sil's vision of a perfectly ordered universe. The city is filled with clockwork automatons and features complex technology inspired by the Dwemer, though it far surpasses anything seen in Tamriel. It is a place of wonder and mystery, with its mechanical inhabitants and intricate designs reflecting Sotha Sil’s obsession with order and control. Few mortals have ever set foot inside the Clockwork City, and it remains one of the most enigmatic locations in Tamriel.
82.name: Clockwork City

84.content: The Ashlanders are nomadic tribes of Dunmer who live in the ash-covered regions of Vvardenfell, Morrowind. Unlike the settled Dunmer of the Great Houses, Ashlanders reject the Tribunal and the structures of civilization, instead adhering to ancient traditions and the worship of their ancestors. Their culture is steeped in reverence for the land and their way of life, which they see as untainted by the corruption of the Tribunal and foreign influence. The Ashlanders live in small, mobile camps and hold a deep respect for their tribal leaders and wise women. They played a significant role in the prophecy of the Nerevarine, who was said to unite the tribes and challenge the rule of the Tribunal.
84.name: Ashlanders

85.content: Nirn is the mortal plane, the central planet in the Mundus, and the world on which Tamriel, Akavir, Atmora, and Yokuda are located. Created by the Aedra at the behest of Lorkhan, Nirn is the physical realm where mortals dwell. It is part of a larger cosmology, with Oblivion and Aetherius existing as the outer realms, where the Daedra and Aedra reside, respectively. Nirn is a place of great beauty, diversity, and strife, as it is home to countless civilizations, cultures, and species. The planet's creation is tied to the sacrifice of Lorkhan, and it serves as the battleground for the struggles between divine and mortal forces, as well as between Aedra and Daedra.
85.name: Nirn

86.content: Tonality, or tonal architecture, is the advanced form of magic and technology used by the Dwemer, the Lost Race of Tamriel. This practice allowed the Dwemer to manipulate reality through sound and vibration, creating powerful artifacts, machines, and constructs. Tonal magic was most famously employed by Kagrenac, the chief tonal architect of the Dwemer, in his creation of the Anumidium, a massive golem designed to achieve godhood. Through tonal manipulation, the Dwemer were able to alter the very fabric of existence, though their experiments ultimately led to their mysterious disappearance from Tamriel. The secrets of tonal magic remain hidden, with few ever understanding its true potential.
86.name: Tonality

87.content: The Skaal are an ancient and isolated tribe of Nords living in the northern reaches of Solstheim. They follow a deeply spiritual way of life, worshipping the All-Maker, a deity that represents the creation force of the universe. Their religion emphasizes living in harmony with nature and the balance of all things in Nirn. The Skaal reject the more traditional Nord worship of the Divines or Daedra, focusing instead on honoring the land and the spirits that inhabit it. Their strict adherence to tradition makes them an isolated and unique people, with rituals and customs tied closely to the harsh yet beautiful environment of Solstheim.
87.name: Skaal

88.content: The Imperial Cult is the state religion of the Empire of Tamriel, dedicated to the worship of the Nine Divines, the gods who helped shape the world of Nirn. This includes the veneration of Akatosh, the Dragon God of Time, and Talos, the god-emperor Tiber Septim, among others. The cult operates temples and shrines across the Empire, providing healing, spiritual guidance, and support to the populace. It is also a political institution, used by the Empire to promote unity among its subjects and strengthen loyalty to the Emperor. The Imperial Cult emphasizes duty, piety, and the moral code that reflects the ideals of the Empire.
88.name: Imperial Cult

89.content: The Divines, or the Nine Divines, are a pantheon of gods worshipped throughout Tamriel. They consist of Akatosh, Arkay, Dibella, Julianos, Kynareth, Mara, Stendarr, Talos, and Zenithar, who are revered for their roles in the creation and ongoing protection of Nirn. Each Divine represents different aspects of life and the world, from Akatosh's control over time to Mara’s influence over love and family. The worship of the Divines is central to the culture of the Empire, and their temples and shrines are found throughout Tamriel, offering spiritual guidance, blessings, and healing. Talos, once the mortal Tiber Septim, holds a unique place as a Divine who ascended to godhood after unifying Tamriel.
89.name: Divines

90.content: Mankar Camoran was the enigmatic and powerful Altmer leader of the Mythic Dawn, a Daedric cult that worshipped Mehrunes Dagon. Mankar Camoran authored the Mysterium Xarxes, a tome of Daedric knowledge that provided instructions for opening the Oblivion Gates and initiating the Oblivion Crisis. He preached that Nirn was a prison created by the Divines and that the Daedra were the true liberators of mortal souls. His teachings led his followers to assassinate Emperor Uriel Septim VII and set off a chain of events that nearly brought Tamriel to ruin. Camoran believed himself to be the rightful ruler of Tamriel and sought to reshape the world in his image by bringing Dagon's destruction to the mortal plane.
90.name: Mankar Camoran

91.content: The Mysterium Xarxes is a powerful and forbidden Daedric artifact written by Mehrunes Dagon, the Daedric Prince of Destruction. It is a book of dark knowledge, containing secrets that can unlock immense power and open portals to the realm of Oblivion. The text of the Xarxes is incomprehensible to most mortals, filled with cryptic symbols and Daedric incantations. Mankar Camoran used the Mysterium Xarxes to lead the Mythic Dawn and trigger the Oblivion Crisis by summoning Mehrunes Dagon into the world. The Xarxes is considered a dangerous and corrupting object, as it holds the potential to unravel the fabric of reality and grant access to the destructive forces of Oblivion.
91.name: Mysterium Xarxes

92.content: The Deadlands are the fiery, hellish realm of Oblivion ruled by Mehrunes Dagon, the Daedric Prince of Destruction. The landscape of the Deadlands is dominated by molten lava, jagged volcanic rocks, and perpetual storms of fire. It is a realm of chaos, where the forces of destruction and change are constantly at work. The Deadlands were the focal point of the Oblivion Crisis, as Dagon's forces invaded Tamriel through Oblivion Gates, attempting to reshape the mortal world in his image of devastation. The realm is home to legions of Daedra, including Dremora and Xivilai, who serve Dagon’s violent ambitions.
92.name: Deadlands

93.content: The Aedra, often referred to as the Divines, are the god-like beings who played a pivotal role in the creation of Nirn and the mortal realm, Mundus. Unlike the Daedra, the Aedra gave up much of their power during the creation process, and as a result, they are more distant from the affairs of mortals. Their sacrifice during the creation of Mundus was significant, especially for Lorkhan, whose heart was used to give life to Nirn. The Aedra are worshipped across Tamriel as part of the pantheon of the Nine Divines, with Akatosh, Mara, and Arkay being some of the most revered among them. The Aedra are associated with order, stability, and the mortal world, in contrast to the Daedra’s chaos.
93.name: Aedra

94.content: The Daedra are powerful, god-like beings who inhabit the planes of Oblivion. Unlike the Aedra, the Daedra did not participate in the creation of Nirn and, therefore, retain their full power. The Daedric Princes, the most powerful of the Daedra, each rule over their own realms of Oblivion, embodying aspects such as destruction (Mehrunes Dagon), madness (Sheogorath), and domination (Molag Bal). The Daedra are often associated with chaos, unpredictability, and a disregard for mortal life. Despite their malevolent reputation, many mortals form cults to worship and seek favors from the Daedra, though these dealings often come at great personal cost. The Daedra are both feared and revered, representing the darker forces at play in Tamriel.
94.name: Daedra

95.content: Molag Bal is the Daedric Prince of Domination, Corruption, and Enslavement. His ultimate goal is the subjugation of all mortals, and he is infamous for his role in the creation of vampires, whom he sees as his children. Molag Bal rules over the plane of Oblivion known as Coldharbour, a twisted and desolate realm that mirrors Nirn, but in a corrupted and decayed state. He is one of the most malevolent and dangerous Daedric Princes, seeking to dominate both souls and physical beings alike. His followers often seek power through dark rituals, offering up the souls of mortals to their cruel master in exchange for corrupt boons.
95.name: Molag Bal

96.content: Hermaeus Mora, also known as the Daedric Prince of Knowledge and Memory, is an ancient being who governs the pursuit of forbidden and hidden knowledge. His realm, Apocrypha, is a vast library containing every piece of information, truth, and secret ever known. Hermaeus Mora is often depicted as a swirling mass of tentacles, representing his endless reach into the unknown. While many seek his favor to gain knowledge, Mora’s gifts are dangerous, often leading to madness or corruption. His followers, including those who use the Black Books, tread a perilous path in exchange for the ancient, arcane secrets he offers. Mora’s influence spans across all of Tamriel, quietly pulling strings from the shadows.
96.name: Hermaeus Mora

97.content: Apocrypha is the Daedric realm of Hermaeus Mora, a dark, eerie library filled with forbidden knowledge. The realm is composed of endless halls lined with ancient tomes, scrolls, and cryptic manuscripts. The walls shift, and the landscape warps, creating an unsettling maze that ensnares those who dare seek its hidden truths. Within Apocrypha, mortals who read the Black Books can gain powerful knowledge and abilities, though often at the cost of their sanity. The seekers and lurkers, dark creatures who serve Mora, guard the secrets of Apocrypha, ensuring that only those deemed worthy—or foolish enough—can unlock its mysteries. Apocrypha is both a treasure trove of knowledge and a trap for those who underestimate its dangers.
97.name: Apocrypha

98.content: Ulfric Stormcloak is the Jarl of Windhelm and the leader of the Stormcloak Rebellion in Skyrim. A veteran of the Great War and a powerful user of the Thu’um, Ulfric became a symbol of Nordic pride and resistance against the Empire after he killed High King Torygg in a duel, sparking the Skyrim Civil War. Ulfric opposes the Empire’s rule and its ban on Talos worship, seeing it as a betrayal of Skyrim's traditions and independence. He seeks to become the High King of Skyrim and lead his people to sovereignty, free from the influence of the Empire and the Thalmor. However, Ulfric’s motivations are questioned by some, with accusations of ambition and self-interest clouding his role as a liberator.
98.name: Ulfric Stormcloak

99.content: The Stormcloaks are a faction of rebels in Skyrim, led by Ulfric Stormcloak, who fight for Skyrim’s independence from the Empire. Their primary grievances include the Empire’s ban on the worship of Talos, which many Nords see as an affront to their cultural and religious identity. The Stormcloaks believe that Skyrim should be ruled by Nords, free from the influence of the Thalmor and the Imperial government. Their rebellion began when Ulfric killed High King Torygg in a challenge for the throne, leading to a civil war that split Skyrim in two. While many Nords support the Stormcloak cause, others see them as extremists whose rebellion could weaken Skyrim and make it vulnerable to outside threats.
99.name: Stormcloaks

100.content: The Skyrim Civil War is a conflict between the Empire and the Stormcloaks, a rebel faction led by Ulfric Stormcloak, who seeks to free Skyrim from Imperial rule. The war began after Ulfric killed High King Torygg, claiming the throne for himself and challenging the legitimacy of Imperial control over Skyrim. The conflict is deeply rooted in the ban on Talos worship, enforced by the White-Gold Concordat, which many Nords see as an unacceptable infringement on their rights and traditions. The war has divided Skyrim, with some supporting the Empire for its stability and others backing the Stormcloaks for their fight for independence. The outcome of the war will determine the future of Skyrim’s place within the Empire.
100.name: Skyrim Civil War

101.content: Talos, also known as Tiber Septim, was a mortal who ascended to godhood after uniting Tamriel under the Septim Empire. As one of the Nine Divines, Talos is revered as the god of war, governance, and heroic action. His legacy is central to the identity of the Empire, with many believing that his divine power is tied to the stability of the realm. However, after the signing of the White-Gold Concordat, the worship of Talos was banned by the Empire to appease the Aldmeri Dominion, who consider Talos worship heresy. This ban has been a source of tension, particularly in Skyrim, where many Nords continue to venerate Talos as their divine protector and a symbol of independence.
101.name: Talos

102.content: Paarthurnax is an ancient and wise dragon who resides atop the Throat of the World, the highest mountain in Tamriel. Once a lieutenant of Alduin, the World-Eater, Paarthurnax betrayed his kin and aided the ancient Nords by teaching them the Thu’um, or the Way of the Voice, which they used to defeat Alduin. Over the millennia, Paarthurnax has lived in seclusion, meditating on the nature of power and the struggle between good and evil. He is a mentor to the Greybeards and plays a crucial role in guiding the Dragonborn in their battle against Alduin. Despite his wisdom and guidance, Paarthurnax's past as a destructive force causes conflict with the Blades, who demand his death as penance for his actions.
102.name: Paarthurnax

103.content: Numidium is a massive, god-like brass golem created by the Dwemer, also known as the Brass Tower. Built by the Dwemer Tonal Architect Kagrenac, Numidium was designed as a tool for the Dwemer to achieve godhood, though its activation contributed to their mysterious disappearance. The golem was later used by Tiber Septim to unify Tamriel, powered by the Mantella, an artificial soul gem. Numidium possesses incredible power, able to reshape reality itself, and it played a critical role in the events known as the Warp in the West. The Numidium’s nature as both a weapon and a divine construct has made it a subject of fascination and fear across Tamriel.
103.name: Numidium

104.content: Arkay is one of the Nine Divines, revered as the god of life and death. He governs the natural cycles of birth, death, and rebirth, and is closely associated with the protection of the souls of the dead. Arkay’s priests oversee funerary rites, ensuring that the dead are properly honored and their souls can pass peacefully into the afterlife. Many in Tamriel call upon Arkay to bless newborns, protect the deceased, and ensure that necromancers cannot desecrate the dead. His influence is felt throughout Tamriel, particularly in times of mourning and remembrance. As a god of balance, Arkay is revered for maintaining the natural order between life and death.
104.name: Arkay

105.content: Dibella is the goddess of beauty, love, and artistic expression, and one of the Nine Divines. She is worshipped across Tamriel for her influence over art, romance, and sensuality. Her followers, the devotees of Dibella, believe that love and beauty should be celebrated in all its forms, whether through art, dance, music, or personal relationships. Temples dedicated to Dibella can be found throughout the Empire, where the faithful practice rituals honoring love and passion. Dibella's influence extends not just to romantic love but also to the appreciation of beauty in all things, from the natural world to the creative expressions of mortals.
105.name: Dibella

106.content: Julianos is the god of wisdom, logic, and knowledge, and one of the Nine Divines. He is the patron of mages, scholars, and teachers, overseeing the pursuit of learning and the study of law. His influence encourages the rational understanding of the world and the practice of magic through knowledge and discipline. Temples dedicated to Julianos often serve as places of study, libraries, and repositories of magical tomes. His followers believe that through education, mortals can improve both themselves and the world around them, making Julianos a favored deity among intellectuals, mages, and philosophers across Tamriel.
106.name: Julianos

107.content: Kynareth, also known as Kyne to the Nords, is the goddess of the sky, nature, and the winds, and one of the Nine Divines. She is associated with the forces of nature and is believed to have played a crucial role in the creation of Nirn. Her influence extends to the natural world, including the weather, wildlife, and the protection of the earth. Kynareth is especially revered by those who live close to nature, such as farmers, hunters, and sailors, who pray for her blessings in their daily lives. Her temples are often located in natural settings, reflecting her connection to the land and the sky. In Nordic tradition, she is also associated with the Thu’um, the Voice, and is revered as the wife of Shor.
107.name: Kynareth

108.content: Mara is the goddess of love, compassion, and family, and one of the most beloved of the Nine Divines. She is associated with the sanctity of marriage, the bond of family, and the nurturing of relationships. Her worshipers often seek her blessings for a happy and prosperous marriage, and Mara’s temples are frequently the sites of weddings throughout Tamriel. She is also revered as a goddess of mercy and healing, offering comfort to the sick and the suffering. Mara's teachings emphasize kindness, charity, and the importance of community, making her a favored deity for those who wish to live in peace and harmony with others.
108.name: Mara

109.content: Stendarr is the god of mercy, compassion, and righteous justice, one of the Nine Divines. He is worshipped as a protector of the weak, the sick, and the oppressed, and his followers seek to uphold his values by showing kindness and charity to those in need. However, Stendarr is also known for his intolerance of evil and injustice, and his priests, known as the Vigilants of Stendarr, are dedicated to hunting down Daedra worshipers, vampires, and other malevolent forces in Tamriel. Stendarr’s mercy is balanced by his commitment to justice, making him a revered deity for healers, protectors, and those who seek to punish wrongdoers in defense of the innocent.
109.name: Stendarr

110.content: Zenithar is the god of work, commerce, and craftsmanship, and one of the Nine Divines. He is often associated with industry, prosperity through honest labor, and the rewards of hard work. Worshippers of Zenithar include merchants, artisans, blacksmiths, and laborers who seek his blessing for success in trade and craftsmanship. Temples dedicated to Zenithar are often found in bustling trade cities and industrial centers, where his followers promote the value of integrity and productivity. His tenets encourage fair trade, cooperation, and earning wealth through virtuous and honest means. Zenithar's symbol is a balance of wealth and morality, embodying the ideals of hard work and prosperity.
110.name: Zenithar

111.content: Morihaus, also known as the Bull of Kyne, is a demigod and one of the most revered figures in the Alessian Rebellion against the Ayleid rulers of Cyrodiil. He was a powerful champion of Saint Alessia and played a critical role in leading the rebellion that liberated humanity from the rule of the Ayleid elves. Morihaus is often depicted as a figure with the strength of a bull, and legends suggest that he was a minotaur, embodying immense physical power and divine favor. He is remembered for his fierce loyalty to Alessia and his role in the founding of the First Empire of Cyrodiil. His legacy is celebrated as a symbol of strength, courage, and liberation.
111.name: Morihaus

112.content: Reman Cyrodiil was the first Emperor of the Second Empire of Tamriel and the founder of the Reman Dynasty. He is regarded as one of the greatest rulers in Tamriel's history, uniting much of the continent under Imperial rule and initiating a period of significant expansion and prosperity. Reman's rise to power began after he successfully repelled the Akaviri invasion, earning him the title of Dragonborn and the loyalty of the Akaviri forces. His reign established the foundations of the Second Empire, which brought peace and stability to Tamriel for centuries. Reman’s legacy as a military strategist and a unifier of nations remains a source of pride for Cyrodiil and the Empire.
112.name: Reman
